title: networking broken in rawhide?
body:
Trivially reproduces on rawhide (f37):
```console
# podman run -d --name foo  -p 127.0.0.1:52515:52515 quay.io/libpod/testimage:20210610 nc -l -n -v -p 52515
b7b068b6b8b313c087d4e7c884f92c229b1130cdf24a6e4d0b687b9ab85a86fb
# echo hi | nc 127.0.0.1 52515     <--- returns immediately, i.e., does not hang or time out
# podman logs foo
listening on [::]:52515 ...
connect to [::ffff:10.88.0.29]:52515 from [::ffff:10.88.0.1]:57764 ([::ffff:10.88.0.1]:57764)
```
(expected to see `hi`).

Note that the command-line `nc` returns immediately (as opposed to hanging/timeout, which is what we see when there's a `cni-podman0` interface on the system). 

On the slim chance that it's helpful:
```
# ip a
1: lo: <LOOPBACK,UP,LOWER_UP> mtu 65536 qdisc noqueue state UNKNOWN group default qlen 1000
    link/loopback 00:00:00:00:00:00 brd 00:00:00:00:00:00
    inet 127.0.0.1/8 scope host lo
       valid_lft forever preferred_lft forever
    inet6 ::1/128 scope host 
       valid_lft forever preferred_lft forever
2: eth0: <BROADCAST,MULTICAST,UP,LOWER_UP> mtu 1500 qdisc fq_codel state UP group default qlen 1000
    link/ether fa:16:3e:77:54:28 brd ff:ff:ff:ff:ff:ff
    altname enp0s3
    altname ens3
    inet 10.0.137.112/22 brd 10.0.139.255 scope global dynamic noprefixroute eth0
       valid_lft 35237sec preferred_lft 35237sec
    inet6 2620:52:0:88:f816:3eff:fe77:5428/64 scope global dynamic noprefixroute 
       valid_lft 2591895sec preferred_lft 604695sec
    inet6 fe80::f816:3eff:fe77:5428/64 scope link noprefixroute 
       valid_lft forever preferred_lft forever
```

aardvark-dns-1.0.3-1.fc37.x86_64
netavark-1.0.3-3.fc37.x86_64

This is causing gating-test failures for podman on f37.

comments.body: I am unable to reproduce on rawhide. What is the podman version you are using?
The one we were discussing yesterday: [4.1.0-2.fc37](https://bodhi.fedoraproject.org/updates/FEDORA-2022-874912dc1e)
Yeah I was not able to reproduce with that. I currently run the full bats test suite, maybe something in there trigger this.

Followup: the problem is `nmap-ncat` (on the host). The `netcat` rpm works fine. nmap-ncat is the same version (7.92) on f35 and f37, just different builds. It works fine on my f35 laptop. Here is the diff of -v-v-v-v:
```
Ncat: Version 7.92 ( https://nmap.org/ncat )                                    Ncat: Version 7.92 ( https://nmap.org/ncat )
libnsock nsock_set_loglevel(): Set log level to DEBUG                           libnsock nsock_set_loglevel(): Set log level to DEBUG
NCAT DEBUG: Using system default trusted CA certificates and those in           NCAT DEBUG: Using system default trusted CA certificates and those in 
NCAT DEBUG: Unable to load trusted CA certificates from /usr/share/nca    |     NCAT DEBUG: Unable to load trusted CA certificates from /usr/share/nca
libnsock nsock_iod_new2(): nsock_iod_new (IOD #1)                               libnsock nsock_iod_new2(): nsock_iod_new (IOD #1)
libnsock event_new(): event_new (IOD #1) (EID #8)                               libnsock event_new(): event_new (IOD #1) (EID #8)
libnsock nsock_connect_tcp(): TCP connection requested to 127.0.0.1:52          libnsock nsock_connect_tcp(): TCP connection requested to 127.0.0.1:52
libnsock nsock_pool_add_event(): NSE #8: Adding event (timeout in 1000          libnsock nsock_pool_add_event(): NSE #8: Adding event (timeout in 1000
libnsock nsock_loop(): nsock_loop() started (no timeout). 1 events pen          libnsock nsock_loop(): nsock_loop() started (no timeout). 1 events pen
libnsock nsock_trace_handler_callback(): Callback: CONNECT SUCCESS for          libnsock nsock_trace_handler_callback(): Callback: CONNECT SUCCESS for
Ncat: Connected to 127.0.0.1:52515.                                             Ncat: Connected to 127.0.0.1:52515.
libnsock nsock_iod_new2(): nsock_iod_new (IOD #2)                               libnsock nsock_iod_new2(): nsock_iod_new (IOD #2)
libnsock event_new(): event_new (IOD #1) (EID #18)                              libnsock event_new(): event_new (IOD #1) (EID #18)
libnsock nsock_read(): Read request from IOD #1 [127.0.0.1:52515] (tim          libnsock nsock_read(): Read request from IOD #1 [127.0.0.1:52515] (tim
libnsock nsock_pool_add_event(): NSE #18: Adding event (timeout in -16    |     libnsock nsock_pool_add_event(): NSE #18: Adding event (timeout in -16
libnsock event_new(): event_new (IOD #2) (EID #26)                              libnsock event_new(): event_new (IOD #2) (EID #26)
libnsock nsock_readbytes(): Read request for 0 bytes from IOD #2 [peer          libnsock nsock_readbytes(): Read request for 0 bytes from IOD #2 [peer
libnsock nsock_pool_add_event(): NSE #26: Adding event (timeout in -16    |     libnsock nsock_pool_add_event(): NSE #26: Adding event (timeout in -16
libnsock event_delete(): event_delete (IOD #1) (EID #8)                         libnsock event_delete(): event_delete (IOD #1) (EID #8)
libnsock nsock_trace_handler_callback(): Callback: READ SUCCESS for EI    <
libnsock event_new(): event_new (IOD #1) (EID #35)                        <
libnsock nsock_write(): Write request for 3 bytes to IOD #1 EID 35 [12    <
libnsock nsock_pool_add_event(): NSE #35: Adding event (timeout in -16    <
libnsock event_delete(): event_delete (IOD #2) (EID #26)                  <
libnsock nsock_trace_handler_callback(): Callback: WRITE SUCCESS for E    <
libnsock event_new(): event_new (IOD #2) (EID #42)                        <
libnsock nsock_readbytes(): Read request for 0 bytes from IOD #2 [peer    <
libnsock nsock_pool_add_event(): NSE #42: Adding event (timeout in -16    <
libnsock event_delete(): event_delete (IOD #1) (EID #35)                  <
libnsock nsock_trace_handler_callback(): Callback: READ EOF for EID 42    <
libnsock event_delete(): event_delete (IOD #2) (EID #42)                  <
libnsock nsock_trace_handler_callback(): Callback: READ EOF for EID 18          libnsock nsock_trace_handler_callback(): Callback: READ EOF for EID 18
libnsock event_delete(): event_delete (IOD #1) (EID #18)                        libnsock event_delete(): event_delete (IOD #1) (EID #18)
Ncat: 3 bytes sent, 0 bytes received in 0.01 seconds.                     |     Ncat: 0 bytes sent, 0 bytes received in 0.07 seconds.
                                                                          >     libnsock nsock_trace_handler_callback(): Callback: READ KILL for EID 2
                                                                          >     libnsock event_delete(): event_delete (IOD #2) (EID #26)
libnsock nsock_iod_delete(): nsock_iod_delete (IOD #1)                          libnsock nsock_iod_delete(): nsock_iod_delete (IOD #1)
libnsock nsock_iod_delete(): nsock_iod_delete (IOD #2)                          libnsock nsock_iod_delete(): nsock_iod_delete (IOD #2)
```
(f35-good on the left, f37-bad on the right). The interesting part is the missing READ and WRITE SUCCESS.
For future readers, the problem is caused by this patch: https://src.fedoraproject.org/rpms/nmap/c/5a4dbdf721c79fdcfc75cd55a72b1be1bb2db5ca?branch=rawhide
...and the bug is being tracked in [rhbz2082270](https://bugzilla.redhat.com/show_bug.cgi?id=2082270). Nice detective work @Luap99 .
Let's leave this open: if they don't fix the `nc` bug, we will have to panic-scramble to find a new way to run tests.

[nmap-ncat-7.92-4.fc37](https://bodhi.fedoraproject.org/updates/FEDORA-2022-384217e6a0) is now in stable. Podman [gating tests](https://bodhi.fedoraproject.org/updates/FEDORA-2022-874912dc1e) passed simply by retriggering & picking up the new build. Life is good.
